youtube.titulo: 9 FAMOSOS que MUDARAM a APARÊNCIA ao USAR MOUNJARO e OZEMPIC

facebook.texto: I had planned everything. The hotel. The booking at the restaurant we used to go to in the early years. The new dress I'd ordered three weeks before and never tried on, because I couldn't face seeing how it looked. Twenty years. This was supposed to be the evening we would still talk about in another twenty. ======== But when the moment came — when he looked at me, when he took my hand — I withdrew. Not physically. Inwardly. I found a reason to get up. To turn the light off. To look away. And th

analise_criativa.avatar: Middle-aged couple (man and woman) shown together outdoors, appearing happy — likely representing a married couple in their 40s-50s. The woman is in a light purple/lavender top. The image is partially visible on the right side of the ad.
analise_criativa.editing: Static image ad with a long-form emotional narrative copy block on the left, and a partially cropped lifestyle couple image on the right. Text is formatted in short paragraphs for readability. The image carries an 'Ozempic available' overlay text and a 'Lose we[ight]' CTA button. Overall style is editorial/advertorial.
analise_criativa.observation: The image is partially cut off, making it hard to fully evaluate the visual. The brand references 'Ozempic' and 'www.ozempil.co.uk' in the headline/URL area, which walks close to medical/drug territory but is framed as a 'natural alternative.' The copy is deeply emotional and identity-driven, focusing on body confidence shame during a romantic anniversary — strong psychological trigger for the female demographic.
analise_criativa.highlight: The copy angle is exceptionally strong — it uses an intimate, shame-based emotional narrative (avoiding intimacy on a 20th anniversary due to body image) that is highly relatable for the core female demographic aged 40-60. This story-driven approach tied to a relationship and self-worth trigger is a proven DR hook. Worth testing the copy angle in video/UGC format with a real female avatar telling the same story.

facebook.texto: I had planned everything. The hotel. The booking at the restaurant we used to go to in the early years. The new dress I'd ordered three weeks before and never tried on, because I couldn't face seeing how it looked. Twenty years. This was supposed to be the evening we would still talk about in another twenty. ======== But when the moment came — when he looked at me, when he took my hand — I withdrew. Not physically. Inwardly. I found a reason to get up. To turn the light off. To look away. And th

analise_criativa.avatar: Middle-aged couple (man and woman) shown together outdoors, appearing happy — likely representing a married couple in their 40s-50s. The woman is in a light blue top. The image is partially visible on the right side of the ad.
analise_criativa.editing: Static image ad with identical long-form emotional narrative copy as Ad 1. The only visible difference is the couple image variant — the woman appears to be wearing a different color top (blue vs purple in Ad 1) and the Library ID differs, suggesting this is an A/B image split test of the same copy. Same advertorial/editorial layout with 'Ozempic available' overlay and 'Lose we[ight]' CTA.
analise_criativa.observation: This ad appears to be a direct split test variant of Ad 1 — same copy, same page, same launch date, same URL, only the couple image differs slightly. Both ads are running simultaneously on the same page, confirming an image A/B test strategy. The Ozempic brand mention in the image overlay is a compliance risk worth flagging.
analise_criativa.highlight: Confirms the brand is actively split testing image variants with the same copy — standard DR optimization behavior. The emotional anniversary narrative is the real asset being tested here. If one image outperforms, it signals which couple archetype resonates more with the target audience. Worth monitoring which variant wins.

repeated_creatives.description: Both ads share identical headline copy ('I had planned everything. The hotel. The booking at the restaurant we used to go to in the early years. The new dress I'd ordered three weeks before and never tried on, because I couldn't face seeing'), the same format (Texto em Tela), the same keyword (natural ozempic), the same run duration (15 days), and the same page (Purely Well UK). The only visible difference is the woman's top color (lavender vs light blue), suggesting these are slight creative variants — likely the same underlying story/video with a cropped or swapped visual to test thumbnail or image color appeal.

frequent_avatars.type: Middle-aged woman (40s-50s) in a relationship context — emotionally invested in her appearance for a romantic occasion, experiencing body image shame that is preventing her from enjoying life milestones

facebook_cross_analysis.dominant_mechanism: Natural Ozempic — positioning a supplement as a plant-based or food-derived GLP-1 analog that mimics the appetite-suppressing and metabolic effects of semaglutide without a prescription
facebook_cross_analysis.emerging_angle: Romantic milestone sabotage — the narrative hook of a woman who planned a meaningful anniversary or date event but was emotionally blocked from participating fully due to body image shame (the untried dress), tapping into relationship preservation and emotional loss rather than vanity or health as the primary weight loss motivator
facebook_cross_analysis.market_signal: The UK market is actively testing emotionally-driven, story-first creatives anchored to the 'natural Ozempic' keyword, using split avatar image variants to optimize visual appeal while keeping copy constant — signaling a funnel focused on GLP-1 curiosity traffic with an emotional narrative conversion strategy targeting women in committed relationships.
